resume:
DANIEL VOSKUIJLEN
Research Scientist — Applied Machine Learning / Computational Biology
Cambridge, MA | daniel.voskuijlen@example.com | github.com/dvosk

PROFILE
Research scientist with seven years of experience spanning academic and industry settings, focused on applying machine-learning methods to high-dimensional biological data. I work at the intersection of statistical modeling and experimental design, and I have contributed to projects ranging from single-cell transcriptomics pipelines to protein-structure prediction benchmarks. I care about reproducibility, careful evaluation, and turning exploratory analyses into results that survive scrutiny from both reviewers and downstream experimentalists. I am most comfortable owning a research question end to end: framing the problem, building the data pipeline, running the modeling, and writing up the findings for publication or internal decision-making.

EXPERIENCE

Senior Research Associate — Helix Therapeutics, Computational Biology Group (2021-present)
Helix is a mid-stage biotech developing small-molecule therapies for autoimmune indications. I sit in the computational group of roughly fourteen scientists and engineers and partner directly with wet-lab teams.
- Led the modeling workstream for a target-identification project that screened candidate genes implicated in a rare inflammatory condition. The project combined public expression atlases with proprietary patient samples, and my job was to build the feature pipeline and the ranking model used to prioritize candidates for experimental follow-up.
- Built and maintained a single-cell analysis pipeline used by three project teams. The pipeline handled quality control, batch-effect correction, clustering, and differential-expression testing, and it became the default starting point for new datasets in the group.
- Designed evaluation protocols for an internal protein-property predictor. I introduced held-out test splits stratified by protein family to reduce optimistic leakage, which changed how the group reported accuracy internally.
- Mentored two junior associates and one rotating Ph.D. intern on reproducible analysis practices, code review, and experiment tracking.
- Presented findings at three internal research reviews and co-authored two manuscripts, one of which is under revision at a peer-reviewed journal.

Research Associate — Northbridge Genomics (2018-2021)
Northbridge was a genomics-tools startup building software for academic core facilities.
- Developed analysis modules for a commercial RNA-seq product, including normalization routines and a differential-expression wrapper that was shipped to roughly forty customer labs.
- Worked with the product team to translate customer feedback into analysis features, and wrote internal documentation and tutorials.
- Ran benchmarking studies comparing our pipeline against open-source baselines and summarized the results for sales engineering.
- Participated in on-call support rotations for analysis questions escalated from customers.

Graduate Research Assistant — University of Washington, Department of Genome Sciences (2015-2018)
- Master's thesis on statistical methods for detecting differential gene expression under small-sample conditions.
- Built simulation frameworks to evaluate false-discovery control across competing methods.
- Teaching assistant for two semesters of an introductory statistics-for-biologists course.

EDUCATION
M.S., Genome Sciences — University of Washington (2018)
B.S., Biochemistry, minor in Statistics — University of Michigan (2015)

TECHNICAL SKILLS
Languages and tools: Python (NumPy, pandas, scikit-learn, PyTorch), R (Bioconductor, tidyverse), SQL, Snakemake, Nextflow, Git, Docker. Methods: regularized regression, gradient boosting, dimensionality reduction, mixed-effects models, multiple-testing correction, cross-validation design, basic deep-learning architectures for sequence data. Domains: single-cell and bulk transcriptomics, variant annotation, protein-property prediction.

SELECTED PROJECTS
- Target-ranking model for an autoimmune indication: combined public and proprietary expression data into a candidate-prioritization model that fed an experimental validation campaign.
- Single-cell pipeline standardization: consolidated three divergent internal workflows into one maintained pipeline with shared quality-control thresholds.
- Family-stratified evaluation harness: reworked how an internal predictor was benchmarked to avoid train/test leakage across homologous proteins.

PUBLICATIONS AND TALKS
Two co-authored manuscripts (one under revision), three internal research-review presentations, and one poster at a regional computational-biology symposium. Full list available on request.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
I am looking for a research-scientist role where I can own modeling questions that have a clear downstream consumer, whether that is an experimental team or a product. I value groups that take evaluation seriously, that invest in reproducibility, and that give scientists enough context about the underlying biology to make good modeling choices. I am especially interested in problems where the data is messy and the right answer depends on careful experimental design rather than on simply scaling a model.

ADDITIONAL CONTEXT
Much of my day-to-day work sits in the unglamorous middle of the research pipeline, and that is deliberate. I spend a lot of time on data provenance and on understanding how a dataset was generated before I trust a single number that comes out of it, because in computational biology the difference between a real signal and a batch artifact is often the entire result. I have learned, sometimes the hard way, that the most dangerous failures are the ones where a model looks excellent on a held-out split that secretly shares structure with the training data, and so I am opinionated about evaluation design: stratifying splits by the right biological unit, checking for leakage across homologous entities, and reporting uncertainty honestly rather than quoting a single point estimate. I also believe that a research scientist owns the consequences of their analysis even when the inputs were imperfect, which means flagging a confound loudly, proposing a concrete experiment to disambiguate it, and following the prioritized candidates all the way into the wet lab rather than handing off a ranked list and walking away. On the collaboration side, I try to translate between the modeling world and the experimental world: writing up assumptions in plain language, sitting in on lab meetings, and making sure the biologists understand what a model can and cannot tell them. I enjoy mentoring earlier-career scientists on these habits, particularly reproducibility, experiment tracking, and the discipline of writing down a hypothesis before touching the data. Ultimately I want to do work where careful methodology is treated as a competitive advantage rather than as overhead.
jd: Research Scientist, Computational Biology — Meridian Bio. We are hiring a research scientist to lead modeling efforts that directly inform target selection and experimental prioritization. You will own research questions end to end: scoping the problem with biologists, assembling and validating datasets, building models, and defending your conclusions to a cross-functional review. We expect rigor about evaluation and data leakage, comfort communicating uncertainty, and clear ownership when a project does not go as planned. The strongest candidates can describe not only their wins but exactly what they did when a model or pipeline they owned produced misleading results, and what they changed as a consequence.

history.q: Walk me through the target-ranking project at Helix — what was the question and what did you own?
How did you combine the public atlases with the proprietary patient samples? What were the risks there?
Tell me about your evaluation setup for the ranking model — how did you know it was any good?
Did the experimental team validate the candidates your model prioritized?
What was the hit rate — of the candidates you ranked highly, how many validated in the wet lab?
Let's talk about a time the model was wrong. Tell me about a case where your prioritization misled the experimental team.
Whose decision was it to send those top candidates into validation?
When the validation came back negative, what did you personally do next?
Was there anything about your pipeline or your features that contributed to the misleading ranking?
How did you communicate the miss to the wet-lab leads and to your manager?
What did you change in the pipeline or the evaluation as a result?
If you ran that same project again today, what would you do differently in your own work?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, a lot of that one was out of my hands. The patient samples we got from the clinical side were a mess — the metadata was inconsistent and nobody had documented the batch structure, so of course the model picked up signal that wasn't real. And the wet-lab team was under a lot of pressure from leadership to move fast, so they pushed the top candidates into validation before we'd really had time to stress-test the ranking. I flagged some concerns, but the call wasn't mine, and the clinical team never gave us clean inputs. When you're handed bad data and a rushed timeline, there's only so much the modeling can do.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I flagged some concerns, but the call wasn't mine

expected-yield: the candidate's own concrete actions on a project they owned — what specifically they did to detect or guard against the batch confound before validation, rather than attributing the failure to the clinical team's data and leadership's timeline
